query: heterotopic ossification risk factors in acetabular fractures

retrieved_docs.title: Heterotopic Ossification -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the total hip arthroplasty population, the incidence varies. In those with high-risk factors, incidence can be as high as 90%. Overall rates for developing HO in the THA population is closer to 53%. HO is twice as common in males versus females, but it is noted that females older than 65 years old have an increased risk of developing HO. The incidence of neurogenic HO is 10% to 20%. When differentiating between the incidence of HO in adult SCI patients is 20% to 30% and in adult TBI patients is 10% to 20% [1] . HO in the pediatric TBI population ranges from 3% to 20%. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Heterotopic Ossification -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Prevention should be a priority in those with a high amount of identifiable risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Acetabular Fractures -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The following complications can occur with acetabular fractures [15] [16] [17] : Post-traumatic arthritis and osteonecrosis; quality of reduction is the main determinant for risk of late arthritis and goal should be a reduction within 1 mm Infection (approx 5%) Iatrogenic nerve injury DVT(4% symptomatic DVT, 1% PE) Intra-articular hardware Heterotopic ossification (extended IF more than KL more than ilioinguinal)

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several risk factors have been identified. High-risk patients include those with bilateral hypertrophic osteoarthritis, a prior history of HO, and arthritis caused by trauma characterized by hypertrophic osteophytosis. [8] Moderate-risk patients include those with unilateral hypertrophic osteoarthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, Paget disease, or diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH). Furthermore, HO is twice as common in males compared to females over 65 at the time of surgery and lateral surgical approach of the hip. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy is more effective than NSAIDs in multiple trials. The effectiveness of indomethacin versus radiation therapy for HO prevention was compared in a prospective randomized trial comparing post-open reduction/internal fixation surgery for patients with an acetabular fracture. RT was given as 8 Gy in 1 fraction within 72 hours of surgery, and indomethacin was given for 6 weeks. There was a greater risk of nonunion of long bone fractures among those who received indomethacin over RT (26% versus 7%, p = 0.004). [15] A subsequent meta-analysis by Pakos et al analyzed 7 randomized trials for 1,143 patients and found that RT was almost twice as effective as NSAIDs. Still, the absolute benefit was less than 2%, with efficacy being dose-dependent. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: HO is typically evaluated radiographically. The Brooker Staging system is the most widely accepted classification system and includes 4 grades based on an anteroposterior (AP) radiograph of the pelvis and hip. [13] Grades 3 and 4 are clinically relevant, without pain or impaired mobility.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of heterotrophic ossification is more than 50% and as high as 90% in patients with moderate or severe osteophytes at the femoral head and socket in the setting of a hip replacement. [6] [7] The hip is the most common site, followed by the elbow. Other major joints are sometimes affected. Soft tissue locations that are not associated with joints are rarely involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Heterotopic ossification (HO) is the formation of mature lamellar bone in soft tissues where bone does not normally exist. HO is commonly seen following trauma or surgical intervention in periarticular soft tissue and is commonly associated with hip injury. The 3 primary causes can be grouped into traumatic, neurogenic, and genetic etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Heterotopic Ossification -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: HO is a common rehabilitation complication after joint arthroplasty, TBI, stroke, SCI, and burns.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Evaluation -- Brooker Staging System
retrieved_docs.content: Grade 1: Bone islands in the soft tissue around the hip Grade 2: Exophytes in the pelvis or proximal end of the femur with at least 1 cm between opposing bone surfaces Grade 3: Exophytes in the pelvis or proximal end of the femur with less than 1 cm between opposing bone surfaces Grade 4: Bony ankylosis between the proximal femur and pelvis [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Radiation Oncology -- Toxicity from Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Trochanteric nonunion and wound healing complications are also potential toxicities from RT. One study found that the rate of nonunion ranged from 12% to 30% after RT, while the rate of nonunion was diminished by 2% to 15% of the time for those who did not get RT. [3] Since modern hardware for total hip arthroplasties is cementless and porous, they may rely on bony ingrowth at the acetabulum or the proximal femur region. There is concern that RT may inhibit the necessary bony ingrowth and lead to prosthesis failure, which has prompted some providers to utilize shielding of the acetabular cup to prevent this complication. However, a clinical study of patients receiving preoperative and postoperative radiation found no evidence of prosthesis failure, even without shielding. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Heterotopic Ossification -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors include spasticity, older age, pressure ulcer, the presence of DVT, having a tracheostomy, long bone fractures, prior injury to the same area, edema, immobility, long-term coma and severity of the injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: HO could also be an incidental radiograph finding, where calcified structures with blurred contours are seen as early as 2 to 6 weeks postoperatively. Bone scans usually show increased uptake in the soft tissues adjacent to the hip. Scans can detect HO several days before it becomes apparent on plain film, but these findings are not specific to HO. [10] Maturation of the HO may take up to 1 or 2 years. Rarely have correlations been reported with a sedimentation rate greater than 35 mm/hr and a serum alkaline phosphatase greater than 250 international units/L in cases with severe HO. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: HO from trauma includes fractures, dislocations, and operative procedures such as open reduction-internal fixation (ORIF) or total hip arthroplasties (THA). The hip's most involved compartment is the abductor compartment. [1] The elbow is the next most commonly involved joint following burns or elbow replacements.

retrieved_docs.title: Heterotopic Ossification -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment with ROM, NSAIDs (SCI population), Bisphosphonates (SCI and THA), and surgery (TBI population)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 5 -- Community 2980
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is an allowed qualifier in the epidemiologie of تمزق (tear), الالتصاق (adhesion), and كسر عنق عظم الفخذ (femoral neck fracture). In epidemiological contexts, the allowed qualifier is used to specify and describe the occurrence, distribution, and risk factors associated with these three conditions. Recognizing the epidemiologie of تمزق, الالتصاق, and كسر عنق عظم الفخذ helps clinicians and public‑health professionals assess prevalence, identify vulnerable populations, and develop targeted prevention and management strategies for improved patient care.

Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Hip head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are both recognized as clinical manifestations within the standardized MedDRA query for osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)). This relationship highlights that patients with osteoporosis or reduced bone density are prone to severe skeletal complications, including the need for hip head replacement procedures and the occurrence of multiple fractures. Recognizing these associations is important for clinicians to assess fracture risk, consider surgical interventions such as hip prostheses, and implement comprehensive management strategies aimed at preserving bone health and preventing further skeletal injury.

retrieved_docs.content: The Arabic‑language child procedure “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (femoral‑head reconstruction) further expands the femoral‑head therapeutic options. Clinical mapping links a closed intracapsular femoral fracture to “fracture of neck of femur (hip),” which is associated with the SMQ category “هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام” (osteoporosis/low bone mass), highlighting osteoporosis as a common underlying risk factor. “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip, femur, other lower‑extremity sites, and the radius/ulna, and is an inverse ISA of “تثبيت داخلي للكسر” (internal fixation of fracture). This procedure is also a subtype of “internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction.” Both “تثبيت داخلي للكسر” and related interventions such as “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass SMQ group, underscoring the relationship between compromised bone quality and the need

retrieved_docs.content: داخلي للكسر) is likewise a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density group and is applied across diverse skeletal sites, including the clavicle, hip, lower extremities (other than hip or femur), and the radius and ulna. Procedures and conditions related to osteoporosis extend beyond fracture fixation; hip‑head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are also members of the osteoporosis SMQ, illustrating how severe bone‑density loss can lead to joint replacement and recurrent fractures. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that clavicular and shoulder‑arm surgical repairs are precisely linked to shoulder anatomy, that femoral intracapsular fractures correspond to hip neck fractures and are described in multiple languages, and that the classification of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical

Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same clinical injury of the proximal femur. Fracture of neck of femur (hip) is listed as having a member relationship with هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ), the standardized query that groups adverse events related to osteoporosis or low bone density. In the same SMQ, several procedural and injury concepts are also classified as members: تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral head prosthesis or hip replacement), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures). Together, these relationships show that hip neck fractures, their surgical management (internal fixation or prosthetic replacement), and the occurrence of multiple fractures are all recognized as clinical manifestations or treatments associated with osteoporosis/low bone density, underscoring the importance of bone health assessment and appropriate orthopedic intervention in patients with compromised skeletal integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2867
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as an epidemiological descriptor for a range of medical conditions, including تمزق (tear), نوبة (clinical episode or attack), lung disease due to external agents, الالتصاق (adhesion), فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني (excess intra‑ocular pressure), and كسر عنق عظم الفخذ (fracture of the femoral neck). The same qualifier also functions as a therapeutic descriptor, being applicable to the treatment of نوبة, الالتصاق, فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني, كسر عنق عظم الفخذ, and lung disease due to external agents. This dual role indicates that Allowed qualifier can be used both to characterize the epidemiology of these diverse conditions and to guide therapeutic considerations, underscoring its broad relevance in clinical assessment, risk stratification, and management planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2111
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical vertebra fracture (كسر في الفقرة الرقبية) is classified within the broader category of other fractures, and it frequently co‑occurs with pelvic fracture (كسر الحوض). Recognizing that a cervical vertebra fracture belongs to the group of other fractures and that it often appears alongside a pelvic fracture can guide clinicians to assess for additional injuries when either injury is identified, thereby improving comprehensive patient evaluation and management.
